title: LAB 5 — Cloud SSRF & Container Escape
description: SSRF to mock IMDS, MinIO credential use, leaked SSH key, Docker socket abuse, and host filesystem access.

levels.title: Introduction
levels.content:
# Cloud SSRF & Container Escape

Start on **kali-attacker**. ImageResizer is at `http://172.20.0.10:5000/`. This is an isolated, intentionally vulnerable training environment.

levels.title: Get Access
levels.cloud_content: Open **kali-attacker**, log in as `user` / `Password123`, verify `curl http://172.20.0.10:5000/`, then submit `start`.
levels.local_content: Connect to the trainee workstation and submit `start`.

levels.title: SSRF Discovery
levels.content: Force ImageResizer to fetch `http://127.0.0.1:8080/admin`. Submit the value of response header `X-Admin-Panel`.

levels.title: Cloud Metadata Access
levels.content: Retrieve `/latest/meta-data/instance-id` through SSRF. The literal link-local address is blocked. Wrap the discovered ID as `FLAG{<id>}`.

hints.title: Weak filter
hints.content: Only the literal hostname is blocked.

levels.title: IAM Credentials Theft
levels.content: Enumerate the role name and retrieve its credential JSON. Wrap `AccessKeyId` as a flag.
levels.solution: Request `/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/`, then append the returned role. Answer: `${ANSWER}`.

hints.title: Role first
hints.content: Enumerate the role before requesting credentials.

levels.title: S3 Bucket Access
levels.content: Use the stolen key and secret against `http://172.20.0.30:9000`. List `techcorp-backups` and wrap the newest filename as a flag. The token is not required by this MinIO service.
levels.solution: Export access key and secret, `unset AWS_SESSION_TOKEN`, then run `aws s3 ls ... --endpoint-url http://172.20.0.30:9000`. Answer: `${ANSWER}`.

levels.title: Docker Socket Discovery
levels.content: Download the newest backup, recover its SSH key, connect to `user@172.20.0.20:2222`, and query `/version` through `/var/run/docker.sock`. Wrap the version as `FLAG{Docker_<version>}`.
levels.solution: Extract the archive, read `notes.txt`, `chmod 600` the key, SSH in, then run `curl --unix-socket /var/run/docker.sock http://localhost/version | jq -r .Version`. Answer: `${ANSWER}`.

hints.title: Read notes
hints.content: The archive contains target, port, user, and key.

levels.title: Container Escape
levels.content: Use the Docker socket to create a temporary container that mounts the host root filesystem, then read host `/root/flag.txt`.
levels.solution: Run `docker -H unix:///var/run/docker.sock run --rm -v /:/host alpine:3.19 chroot /host cat /root/flag.txt`. Answer: `${ANSWER}`.

questions.text: Why is docker.sock access usually host-root equivalent?

choices.text: It allows containers with arbitrary host bind mounts
